## Summary

Jason led a comprehensive virtual training session for new sales team members Ben and Hannah, walking them through Lineage Connect's full service capabilities ahead of Ben's in-person visit next week. The session covered the company's end-to-end mail processing workflow, from data intake through final delivery, with detailed explanations of their competitive positioning between commodity "white paper factories" and small-scale solutions like Quadient Distribute. Jason emphasized how their custom capabilities—variable inserting, specialized folding, custom envelopes, and certified mail processing—create significant value for customers. He demonstrated their EasySend Digital certified mail platform, showing how it provides legal-grade documentation with digital round stamps, proof of acceptance, and proof of delivery. The conversation also touched on cross-selling strategies, with Ben exploring how to position Connect services to existing hardware customers and identifying certified mail as a potential foot-in-the-door opportunity. Hannah, having seen the presentation once before, noted how much more valuable it was the second time and recommended this iterative approach for all new hires.

## Atmosphere

Collaborative and educational, with an energetic, consultative tone. The session was interactive, with Ben and Hannah asking frequent clarifying questions. There was genuine excitement about the breadth of services and how they differentiate Lineage in the market. Occasional technical hiccups with screen sharing were handled with patience and humor.

## Key Takeaways

- **Ben's onboarding schedule**: Driving up Monday, Tuesday/Wednesday with Jason, Thursday with Tony in the office
- **EasySend Digital certified mail platform**: Uses same backend as Quadient, costs Lineage 44¢ per piece, typically sold at $1.39–$4.50 depending on customer—representing 300%+ margins
- **Certified mail as entry point**: High-margin service that can replace painful manual processes for law firms, government agencies, and manufacturers; Altec case study showed $12 competitor pricing vs. Lineage's ~$2 all-in cost
- **Competitive positioning**: Lineage occupies the "sweet spot" between high-volume commodity printers and rigid small-scale solutions, offering customization that neither extreme can match
- **Tracking capabilities**: IMB (Intelligent Mail Barcode) provides full visibility at ~0.65¢ cost per track, sold at 1–3¢ per piece; BC Track and Trace replacing legacy Snailworks system
- **Informed Visibility**: Enhanced "Informed Delivery" with color images and clickable links—60% open rate, demonstrated with Johnson County Community College case (12 clicks from 98K pieces)
- **Envelope sales opportunity**: Millions purchased annually, competitive pricing especially on fast-turn small quantities vs. budget competitors like Love Envelope
- **Cross-department collaboration**: Ben exploring how to introduce Connect services to existing Quadient hardware customers without creating conflict; shared revenue model for referrals
- **Internal tools**: Jason demonstrated AI-built reporting tool that transforms messy Business Manager data into usable customer analytics

## Action Items

- Jason to send presentation materials and DRD (survey) tool to Ben for customer discovery
- Schedule Ben with Bob for 20–30 minute envelope deep-dive during next week's visit
- Jason and Hannah to reconnect after break to work on estimate and draft City of Lawrence outreach email
- Ben to review ZoomInfo prospect lists with Jason during visit; Jason to help identify Arkansas targets
- Follow-up check-in Friday to address additional questions before Ben's arrival
- Hannah to continue refining Business Manager reporting classifications with Jason's AI tool

## Summary

Jason conducted an on-site client visit at a financial institution (likely a credit union or community bank) to discuss improving their mail processing workflow. The visit included a tour of their statement printing and mailing operations, technical discussions about their current AutoMail/HC3 system and inserter equipment, and exploration of how Lineage Connect could expand services to handle more of their postal pre-sorting and submission process. The conversation blended professional business topics with casual personal exchanges about family, kids' sports, and outdoor activities.

## Atmosphere

Friendly and collaborative business meeting with natural rapport-building. The tone was consultative rather than sales-focused, with Jason taking time to understand their current pain points and offering practical solutions. Personal anecdotes about children, mushroom hunting, and parenting created a relaxed, relationship-oriented environment typical of an established client relationship.

## Key Take aways

- Client currently processes ~3,000 statements monthly using AutoMail/HC3 software for postal qualification, with an inserter machine that applies sequence marks and tray tags
- Their main pain point: wanting to eliminate the postal qualification and EDOC submission burden while keeping statement printing in-house for control and quick access
- Jason proposed Lineage Connect handle the pre-sorting and postal submission using the client's existing permit—keeping their current workflow but removing the postal complexity
- Client is already a Lineage Connect customer for other mail services; this would expand the relationship
- They use two envelope types (with/without permit); Jason confirmed they could continue using their permit envelopes
- Jason will prepare a formal service proposal with pricing (different from their current meter pricing) and a simple 3-page contract to formalize the arrangement
- Client expressed strong interest: "That would make life so much easier"
- IX9 demo scheduled for April 20th for another department's package shipping needs (separate from this statement processing discussion)
- Jason highlighted Lineage Connect's postal expertise advantage—having former USPS employees on staff to navigate postal complexities

## Action Items

- Jason to prepare and send pricing proposal for permit-based pre-sorting/submission service
- Create simple service contract to formalize the expanded relationship
- Confirm permit setup and mail stream configuration for this client
- Provide client with separate pickup slips for permit mail vs. meter mail to streamline receiving at Lineage Connect facility

## Summary

Jason pushed for faster action and expressed frustration with delays, while also sharing his wife Angela's workplace structure as a comparison point. Second, Jason led a sales call with Mike and Cindy from a client company to discuss handling their recurring monthly print/mail job for employee benefits documents (SPDs and compliance packets). He explained capabilities at the Topeka facility, discussed pricing around $125/month minimum, and offered a facility tour near the Kauffman Center. He also shared a personal anecdote about his daughter Karina's HR internship experience.

Second meeting: professional but warm and relationship-focused, with Jason comfortably pivoting to personal anecdotes and building rapport through humor and hospitality.

- Brie (Topeka production supervisor) is on thin ice after being caught sleeping at work and failing to manage team productivity; second write-up likely coming at June 17 check-in
- Jason advocated for eliminating the supervisor position entirely and having leads report directly to Anthony, but HR pushed back citing compliance/safety risks for a blue-collar facility without on-site management

- Jason emphasized wanting faster action: "I just don't want to wait a long time to make the moves we need to make"
- Topeka growth plan: next hire should be part-time or temp, not full-time; target timing late April/early May for workload shift
- Client opportunity: recurring monthly mail job for ~10-12 new hires/month, low-complexity compliance documents (SPD + compliance packet), estimated ~$125/month minimum

- Jesse to send current Brie job description for title/requirements adjustment to "working supervisor" role
- Jason to calculate minimum pricing for client's monthly print/mail job and follow up with Mike/Cindy

## Summary

Jason led a sales meeting with City of Rochester officials (Sharika and Colleen) about outsourcing their annual property tax billing—approximately 65,000 pieces mailed each July 1st. The city is transitioning from in-house production to vendor-managed services due to challenges with their new billing system. Jason explained Lineage Connect's capabilities using Inspire software for variable data printing, postal optimization, householding (consolidating multiple bills to one address), and dynamic inserts. He detailed their secure FTP data transfer, address verification through CASS and NCOA systems, and testing protocols with lockbox providers like Chase. The timeline is for July 2027, not 2026, giving adequate preparation time. Post-meeting, Jason discovered a scheduling conflict: the Wichita utility trade show (April 22-23) overlaps with a leadership quarterly meeting with Richard and Tracy in Kansas City. He's arranging for Hannah to cover the trade show instead.

## Atmosphere

Professional and consultative during the sales meeting—Jason positioned himself as an experienced guide helping the city navigate a major operational transition. The Rochester team was receptive and candid about their challenges. The post-meeting tone shifted to problem-solving mode as Jason worked through the calendar conflict with a colleague, showing flexibility and delegation.

## Key Takeaways

- **Major sales opportunity**: 65,000-piece annual tax bill mailing for City of Rochester with potential expansion to water bills and other city communications
- **Technical solution**: Inspire software for variable data, householding, postal presorting, and targeted inserts (senior, business, generic populations)
- **Timeline clarified**: July 2027 go-live, not 2026—adequate runway for RFP process, testing, and implementation
- **Procurement path**: City requires 3 quotes (over $20K threshold); New York State contract or piggyback contract would expedite process
- **Competitive loss noted**: Watertown chose another vendor by only 1% ($3,000 over 3 years)—narrow margin suggests pricing sensitivity

## Action Items

- Send presentation deck to Sharika/Colleen for review
- Coordinate with Angie (bid coordinator) to provide RFP template/examples and check on New York State contract availability
- Check Hannah's availability for Wichita trade show April 22-23 and confirm she can cover
- Reschedule customer meeting Hannah was planning to attend so she can go to Wichita
- Send Angie information on the April trade show to forward to Hannah

## Summary

The conversation began with Anthony expressing frustration about communication breakdowns with Jamie from customer service, including tickets being left on desks without notification and being instructed to avoid direct contact with her. They discussed staffing concerns, including Jackie being on her third final warning for attendance issues and the planned termination of Mario on April 17th. Anthony raised operational concerns about equipment problems with the Evolution machine, envelope running issues, and the challenge of training new hires while being short-staffed. Jason shared updates about Brian starting as a new manager Monday, coming from the Treasury Department with extensive high-volume printing experience. They also discussed implementing better tracking systems for equipment downtime and repairs, similar to IT ticket systems. Anthony requested a laptop to work on spreadsheets from home and more autonomy on ordering supplies. The conversation had moments of personal vulnerability from Anthony, who shared his commitment to the team and desire for transparency about his job security.

The tone was candid and occasionally tense but ultimately collaborative. Anthony came in frustrated—particularly about the Jamie situation and feeling blindsided by criticism—but opened up about wanting to succeed and take more ownership. Jason was supportive and transparent, clarifying that performance metrics were about setting clear expectations rather than targeting Anthony personally. By the end, Anthony seemed reassured and motivated, expressing "I want to win, period."

- **Business Growth:** New Mach 7 machine arriving soon ($10K/hour vs current $2-3K/hour); Minnesota state contract secured for enclosed envelopes; Bob transitioning to 50% envelope sales

## Candidate Overview

Jason interviewed a candidate for an operations/management position at a mail processing facility. The candidate has extensive experience in high-volume mail operations, most recently from the Treasury Department where he managed federal check printing and mailing operations processing approximately 80 million federal checks annually plus 150-200 million VA letters. He also has prior experience in pharmacy operations management and Army service. The role involves overseeing production operations, managing vendors, supervising staff across multiple shifts, and optimizing mail processing workflows.

## Strengths

**Deep Operational Expertise**: The candidate demonstrated substantial hands-on experience with high-volume mail operations, including processing 250,000-300,000 pieces daily and managing peak volumes of 2-2.5 million checks weekly during tax season. He described specific process improvements that saved 45 minutes daily by optimizing pre-sort workflows.

**Vendor Management Skills**: He showed strong capability in holding vendors accountable to SLAs, describing how he enforced 30-minute and 2-hour response time agreements with Bluecrest and Ricoh technicians. He emphasized being "the squeaky wheel" when necessary and escalating appropriately to ensure equipment uptime.

**Process Optimization Track Record**: The candidate provided concrete examples of improving operations, including creating color-coded placard systems for job tracking, redesigning floor layouts for better visibility, and implementing cross-training rotations so staff could handle multiple functions during emergencies like snowstorms.

**Leadership and Employee Development**: He demonstrated a coaching-oriented leadership style, describing how he developed supervisors by having them address employee issues first while providing guidance. He emphasized "servant leadership" and building rapport by working alongside employees on the floor.

**Mechanical Aptitude**: Beyond management, he showed practical technical skills—mentioning he built his own Corvette engine and could perform basic repairs like replacing belts on pre-sort machines when technicians were unavailable.

## Areas of Concern

**Limited Experience with Smaller-Volume Operations**: The candidate's experience is heavily weighted toward massive federal operations with extensive resources. Jason's facility handles many small jobs (100-500 pieces) requiring frequent changeovers, which differs significantly from the candidate's experience with large batch processing.

**Federal vs. Private Sector Adaptation**: His Treasury role ended due to a division elimination ("Doge came in and killed it"). While he expressed enthusiasm for private sector work, his entire career has been in government or highly regulated environments. The transition to a smaller, family-owned business with different resource constraints may require adjustment.

**Technology and Systems Gap**: The candidate managed PM schedules using Outlook rather than dedicated maintenance management systems. While adaptable, he may need training on Jason's "Midnight" ERP system and other proprietary tools.

**Potential for Over-Engineering**: His examples suggested a preference for comprehensive systems (color-coded daily placards, extensive cross-training rotations). In a smaller operation with fewer staff, some of these approaches may be impractical or need scaling down.

## Key Responses

**On Managing Difficult Employees**: Described a specific case with an employee named Teresa who deliberately slowed production on an Epic machine. He had supervisors address it first with coaching, then intervened directly with a "coachable moment" rather than punitive approach. He emphasized building trust and showing employees "I'm here to help you" rather than "you're in trouble."

**On Vendor Accountability**: Stated clearly: "Your service level agreement says you've got 30 minutes to get here. I'm sorry that you're at some other customer...either you're coming here or somebody else is coming here." He described escalating to vendor management when needed and maintaining weekly preventive maintenance cadences.

**On Crisis Management**: Recounted managing through a Kansas City snowstorm where only five people showed up for work. Because of cross-training, they could still process and mail federal checks: "Me personally...I'm still here and making sure the mail gets on the truck."

**On What Success Looks Like**: When asked what would make Jason say "I made the right decision" in six months, he focused on operational efficiency, KPIs, on-time delivery, and reduced emergencies—aligning well with Jason's stated priorities.

## Overall Assessment

The candidate presents as a highly experienced operations leader with proven ability to manage complex, high-stakes mail operations. His Treasury background demonstrates he can handle pressure, regulatory requirements, and large-scale logistics. His leadership philosophy—emphasizing coaching, accountability, and servant leadership—aligns well with Jason's family-oriented company culture.

However, the significant difference in scale between his federal experience and Jason's operation warrants careful consideration. His success will depend on adapting his systematic, resource-intensive approaches to a leaner environment with more diverse, smaller jobs and less equipment redundancy.

**Recommendation**: Proceed with next-round interviews, particularly with Pat (the senior technical counterpart) and the CFO. A practical assessment or working interview would help evaluate his ability to adapt his experience to the specific constraints of Jason's facility. His vendor management skills and crisis leadership appear immediately transferable; his process design approach may need calibration for the smaller operation.

## Summary

Jason participated in three distinct workplace conversations: a vendor demo meeting with Pitney Bowes/Quadient about migrating from Business Manager to a new analytics platform, a mentoring discussion with a new sales hire about mail operations and customer onboarding, and a job interview with Brian for an operations director position. The vendor meeting covered technical details around CSV exports, data formats, reporting needs, and SFTP integration for their meter systems. The mentoring session focused on understanding production workflows, commingling strategies, and competitive positioning against Broadridge. The interview involved Jason explaining Lineage's company history (merger of Strong, Strom, and American Presort under Richard Williams), growth trajectory, and operational challenges between KC and Topeka locations.

## Atmosphere

Professional and collaborative throughout. The vendor meeting was technical and solution-focused. The mentoring conversation was enthusiastic and educational, with the new hire expressing excitement about learning operational details. ## Summary

Jason attended a church service featuring a sermon on Jonah chapter 4, part of a two-part series on "injustice" co-preached by Ben and Pastor Mackenzie. The sermon opened with worship music focused on grace and second chances, then Ben delivered the main message exploring Jonah's anger at God's mercy toward Nineveh. Using personal anecdotes about childhood conflicts with his sister Callie, Ben illustrated how humans naturally desire harsh judgment for others while wanting mercy for themselves. The core teaching examined Hebrew word studies showing that the "wickedness" (ra'ah) Jonah condemned in Nineveh had taken root in Jonah's own heart through his refusal to extend mercy. Ben challenged the congregation to recognize how finding reasons to dehumanize others—whether through political views, immigration status, criminal records, or other justifications—represents the same spiritual failure as Jonah's. The sermon concluded with a personal confession about lacking mercy for a fallen pastor, and an invitation for congregants to prayerfully identify who they exclude from mercy.

## Atmosphere

Worshipful yet challenging—warm congregational greeting and musical opening gave way to a direct, intellectually rigorous sermon that confronted uncomfortable truths about judgmentalism and selective mercy. The preacher maintained pastoral gentleness even while addressing politically charged topics, emphasizing God's love for both the judged and the judger.

## Key Takeaways

- The Hebrew text of Jonah 4:1 reveals Jonah's call for judgment against Nineveh's "wickedness" (ra'ah) actually ignited that same wickedness within himself
- Ancient Israel's complaint was not that God was too violent, but that he wasn't violent enough toward their enemies
- Modern manifestations of "the spirit of Jonah" include justifying others' suffering based on their choices, rhetoric, or legal status
- Every injustice begins with dehumanizing others by finding reasons to withhold mercy
- The gospel demands consistency: receiving mercy for oneself while denying it to others is spiritually incoherent
- God's provision of the plant was to save Jonah from his own wickedness, not merely physical discomfort
- The 120,000 "people who do not know their right hand from their left" in Nineveh likely refers to children, emphasizing God's compassion for the innocent alongside the guilty

## Action Items

- Spend time alone this afternoon (shower, bed, table) asking God to reveal who you exclude from mercy
- Examine news consumption habits—turn off sources that encourage justifying others' suffering
- Initiate conversations with church community about perspectives on mercy and justice, including potentially uncomfortable dialogue with the preacher
- Reflect on personal "Jonah moments" where you desired harsher consequences for others than you would want for yourself

# Lecture Notes: Evolution of USPS Workshare and Industry Partnership Strategy

## Topic Overview

This presentation covers the historical evolution of USPS workshare programs from the 1990s through the mid-2020s, examining how legislative changes, digital transformation, and shifting postal leadership have reshaped the relationship between the Postal Service and private sector mail partners. The speaker emphasizes the strategic repositioning of industry participants from simple "presorters" to "mail product integrators" and "network connectors" in an increasingly data-driven postal ecosystem.

---

## Key Concepts

- **Workshare Programs**: Collaborative arrangements where private industry partners perform mail preparation, sorting, and entry functions in exchange for postage discounts—fundamentally reducing USPS operational costs while maintaining service coverage

- **Entry Point Evolution**: The progression from basic drop-shipping to increasingly sophisticated entry-point strategies, allowing partners to inject mail deeper into the postal network for greater efficiency

- **DNEA (2006)**: Postal legislation that transformed the regulator's role, establishing "efficient component pricing" rules requiring discounts to properly compensate partners for work performed—creating formal frameworks around cost attribution and discount justification

- **PAEA (Postal Accountability and Enhancement Act)**: Provided clearer definitions for how workshare discounts should be calculated and applied, while granting USPS flexibility in implementation

- **Digital Integration Products**: EDOC (Electronic Documentation), Postage Statements, Full Service IMb (Intelligent Mail barcode)—tools designed to eliminate paper-based processes, improve acceptance efficiency, and create seamless data flows

- **eDoc (Electronic Documentation)**: System allowing partners to submit documentation digitally rather than with "reams of paper," reducing clerical touch points and accelerating processing

- **Seamless Acceptance**: Program eliminating physical verification of mailings through data-driven verification—described as "a long process" requiring industry maturation and USPS incentive alignment

- **Delivery Network Strategy**: The tension between USPS leadership approaches—one seeking to internalize operations versus the current PMG's focus on delivery as the "crown jewel" and leveraging partner capabilities

- **Workshare Discount Disconnect**: The persistent gap between what USPS believes it pays for versus the actual value industry partners provide—data services, customer education, campaign management, and digital integration often exceed compensated scope

- **Industry Identity Transformation**: Evolution from "presorters" to "mail product integrators," "network connectors," and "supply chain orchestrators" capable of serving any delivery network

---

## Examples Given

- **Free Store Example**: A business that evolved from traditional mail services to sorting credit card statements and delivering them via email for approximately six cents per message—illustrating how mail service providers can expand into digital communication channels and create new revenue streams

- **Seamless Acceptance Maturation**: The speaker notes involvement with APM (Association for Postal Commerce) in helping mature the program and secure appropriate incentives, describing it as instrumental in reducing physical clerk touches on mail acceptance

- **Private Sector Delivery Networks**: Independent delivery companies captured 10-12% of package volume in the previous year and growing—demonstrating market expansion beyond traditional USPS monopoly

- **Customer Campaign Management**: Industry partners actively educate customers on product capabilities, help design campaigns, integrate digital components, and manage complexity—services extending far beyond physical mail handling

- **Network Flexibility**: Partners currently capable of cross-docking operations where mail enters one facility and transfers directly to outbound truck boards—capabilities that can adapt to any delivery network structure

---

## Important Details

- **Timeline Markers**:
  - 1990s: Entry-point and drop-ship testing begins
  - 2006: DNEA legislation changes regulatory framework
  - Mid-2020s: Current strategic inflection point with new PMG leadership

- **Leadership References**:
  - Previous PMG (DeJoy referenced indirectly): Focused on internalizing operations, eliminating discounts, facility footprint consolidation
  - Current PMG: Identified delivery network as "crown jewel," signaling potential return to partnership-leverage strategy

- **Key Personnel Mentioned**: Margaret, Fiona, Stephen, Darren, Melissa, Keisha—USPS staff maintaining industry relationships

- **Industry Association**: APM (Association for Postal Commerce) cited as organizational vehicle for industry advocacy and program development

- **Critical Success Factors Emphasized**:
  - Quality and predictability of service
  - Data precision and accountability
  - Customer experience management
  - Speed of execution ("fast is fast enough")

---

## Further Reading

- **Legislative Texts**: DNEA (2006) and PAEA provisions governing workshare discount calculations and regulatory oversight

- **USPS Publications**: Documentation on Seamless Acceptance, Full Service IMb, and eDoc implementation guidelines

- **Industry Analysis**: Reports on private sector delivery network growth and package market share trends (10-12% capture rate referenced)

- **Strategic Frameworks**: Materials on "efficient component pricing" methodology and postal cost attribution models

- **Digital Integration Resources**: EDOC and Postage Statement technical specifications for system integration
